summary: Italian Renaissance polymath (1452–1519). Student of Verrocchio, employed by Ludovico Sforza in Milan. Known for the Mona Lisa (portrait of Lisa del Giocondo, at the Louvre), The Last Supper (Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan), Virgin of the Rocks (two versions), Vitruvian Man, Lady with an Ermine (Cecilia Gallerani), and Salvator Mundi (sold for $450M). Used sfumato technique. Notebooks in left-handed mirror script include the Codex Leicester (owned by Bill Gates). Modeled as Plato in Raphael's School of Athens.

works.name: Mona Lisa
works.description: Portrait of Lisa del Giocondo (wife of Francesco del Giocondo), using sfumato. At the Louvre in its own room; being moved out of Salle des Etats as of January 2025. Stolen in 1911 by Vincenzo Peruggia. Marcel Duchamp's L.H.O.O.Q. defaced it with mustache and goatee. Parodies by Botero (age twelve) and Dalí (photographed by Halsman). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Portrait of a female member of the Giocondo family using sfumato technique; at the Louvre in its own room

draws millions of visitors per year to its private room at the Louvre

works.name: The Last Supper
works.description: Mural in the refectory of Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan, commissioned by Ludovico Sforza. Used oil/tempera on plaster (not true fresco) — began decaying within months; Vasari called it a 'muddle of blots.' Used Jesus's cheek as vanishing point. Thirteen figures all on one side; Judas knocks over a salt cellar; faces in darkness. 15-minute viewing limit. Bombed in 1943 (protected by sandbags). Wikipedia

clues.clue: In the refectory of Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan; commissioned by Ludovico Sforza

works.name: Virgin of the Rocks
works.description: Two versions (Louvre and National Gallery London). Shows John the Baptist as a child kneeling beside a blue-cloaked Mary in a grotto, with angel Uriel in a triangular composition. London version features Stars-of-Bethlehem flowers; grotto inspired by study of Lake Arno's fossil shoreline. Uses sfumato. Wikipedia

works.name: Vitruvian Man
works.description: Drawing of an idealized human figure inside a square and circle (two superimposed positions), inspired by Vitruvius's De architectura. Notes include 'six palms make one cubit' and a line through the navel is '24 palms' long. Inspired Le Corbusier's Modulor system. Wikipedia

works.name: The Battle of Anghiari (lost)
works.description: Lost painting in the Salone dei Cinquecento, Palazzo Vecchio, Florence. Four soldiers fight for a standard. Abandoned when oil-on-wall technique failed (same mistake as Last Supper). May have been painted over by Vasari's Battle of Marciano (phrase 'Cerca Trova'). Rubens's Hippopotamus Hunt modeled on it.

works.name: Lady with an Ermine (Cecilia Gallerani)
works.description: Portrait of Cecilia Gallerani, mistress of Ludovico Sforza, holding an ermine symbolizing her pregnancy. Name given by the Czartoryski family to distinguish it from La Belle Ferronnière. Polished jet necklace references Sforza's nickname 'the Moor.' Wikipedia

works.name: Salvator Mundi
works.description: Portrait of Jesus holding a crystal orb in his left hand and raising two fingers with his right (blessing gesture). Sold for $450.3 million in 2017 to Prince Badr for the Louvre Abu Dhabi — most expensive painting ever sold. Controversially attributed to Leonardo; Walter Isaacson argued the orb is rendered unrealistically. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Leonardo da Vinci (1452–1519) was an Italian Renaissance polymath, painter, sculptor, architect, and inventor. He trained under Andrea del Verrocchio in Florence — reportedly modeled as the Archangel Raphael in Verrocchio's Tobias and the Angel — and assisted Verrocchio in creating the gold-leafed copper orb atop Florence's cathedral. He was later employed by Ludovico Sforza, Duke of Milan, a relationship that produced his most celebrated works.

The Mona Lisa, a portrait of Lisa del Giocondo (a female member of the Giocondo family) using his signature sfumato technique, resides in its own room at the Louvre, where it draws millions annually. It is being moved out of the Salle des Etats as of January 2025. It was stolen in 1911 by Vincenzo Peruggia; Guillaume Apollinaire implicated Picasso. Marcel Duchamp's 1919 L.H.O.O.Q. added a mustache and goatee to a postcard of it (letters mean 'she has hot booty'). Fernando Botero parodied the subject at 'Age Twelve'; Salvador Dali was photographed as its subject by Philippe Halsman. Pascal Cotte's scans revealed the subject originally had visible eyebrows.

The Last Supper, commissioned by Ludovico Sforza for the refectory of Santa Maria delle Grazie in Milan, depicts all thirteen figures on the same side of the table. Leonardo used Jesus's cheek as the vanishing point. He painted it in an oil/tempera mixture on plaster — not true fresco — and it began decaying within months; Vasari, writing less than 60 years later, described it as a 'muddle of blots.' Henry James called it 'the saddest work of art in the world.' It has a 15-minute viewing limit. It was struck by bombs in 1943 (protected by sandbags). Judas's face is in the dark and he knocks over a salt cellar. Andy Warhol's last artwork was sixty silkscreen prints of it. Marisol sculpted herself looking at it; Mary Beth Edelson replaced its figures with women artists.

The Virgin of the Rocks exists in two versions (Louvre and London National Gallery), both showing the angel Uriel, an infant John the Baptist, and the Virgin Mary in a triangular composition set in a rocky grotto. The London version features intricately rendered Stars-of-Bethlehem flowers; the grotto was inspired by Leonardo's study of Lake Arno's fossil shoreline.

The Vitruvian Man depicts an idealized figure inscribed in two superimposed positions inside a square and circle, drawn after Vitruvius's De architectura. Backward notes describe a line through the navel as '24 palms' long. Le Corbusier's Modulor anthropometric system was inspired by it. Hutan Ashrafian has argued the figure was modeled on a corpse.

The Battle of Anghiari is a lost painting depicting four soldiers fighting for a standard, painted in the Salone dei Cinquecento of the Palazzo Vecchio, Florence. Leonardo abandoned it when his oil-on-wall technique failed — the same problem that plagued The Last Supper. It may have been painted over by Vasari's Battle of Marciano (identified by the phrase 'Cerca Trova' on a green flag). Rubens's Hippopotamus Hunt is modeled on it.

Lady with an Ermine depicts Cecilia Gallerani, Ludovico Sforza's mistress, holding an ermine symbolizing her pregnancy with Sforza. The Czartoryski family named it to distinguish it from La Belle Ferronnière (a portrait of Lucretia Crivelli wearing a ferronnière headband).

Salvator Mundi, a portrait of Jesus holding a crystal orb and raising two fingers in blessing, sold for $450.3 million in 2017 to Prince Badr for the Louvre Abu Dhabi — the most expensive painting ever sold. Its attribution to Leonardo is controversial; Walter Isaacson argued the orb is rendered unrealistically, while Martin Kemp defends attribution.

His notebooks run to over 7,000 pages in left-handed mirror script. The Codex Leicester was purchased by Bill Gates in 1994. The notebooks were inherited by Francesco Melzi. Leonardo was the model for the figure of Plato in Raphael's School of Athens (holding Timaeus, pointing upward). Ingres painted his deathbed attended by Francis I. He was teacher to Salai (who modeled for both John the Baptist and the homoerotic Angel in the Flesh drawing). His portrait of Ginevra de' Benci is the only Leonardo publicly viewable in the Americas (National Gallery, Washington). His earliest known commissioned painting was the Annunciation, in which a figure holds a white lily representing Florence. He drew two sketches of Leda with a Swan and produced grotesque head caricatures — the Ugly Duchess by Quentin Matsys may derive from a lost Leonardo grotesque head.
